cot_content:
Okay, so I need to figure out where to get a 2 cm by 10 cm strip of plastic. The options are A. cut out from your ice tray or B. cut out from your ice cream lid.

First, I should think about the typical sizes of ice trays and ice cream lids. An ice tray is usually made of plastic and is used to make ice cubes. The standard ice cubes are about 2-3 cm in size, so if you cut a strip from an ice tray, maybe the thickness would be around 2 cm, and the length could be 10 cm if you measure a part of the tray.

On the other hand, an ice cream lid is the cover that goes over a container of ice cream. These lids are usually flat and might be made of a different type of plastic, maybe thinner. The size of an ice cream lid would depend on the container, but they are typically circular or rectangular but not necessarily 2 cm by 10 cm. It's possible to cut a strip from a lid, but I'm not sure if the dimensions would fit.

Another factor is the thickness of the plastic. Ice trays are often made of thicker plastic to hold the weight of the ice, while ice cream lids might be thinner for protection. The question mentions a "strip" which suggests a flat piece, so either could work, but the dimensions are specific.

If I imagine an ice tray, each compartment is usually around 2 cm in width and depth, so if you take a strip that's 2 cm wide and 10 cm long, it might come from the side of the tray. An ice cream lid, being a flat cover, might not have the same dimensions. The length of 10 cm is about the size of a standard ice cream container's diameter or length, but the 2 cm width is a bit specific.

I think the answer is more likely to be the ice tray (A) because the dimensions match the compartments, but I'm not 100% sure. I'll go with A.
label: minority
